title: Dog Dragging Hind Legs: Causes, Urgency, and Veterinary Triage Guide
meta_desc: If your dog is dragging their back legs, it is a high-priority emergency. Learn about causes like IVDD and how to seek immediate veterinary help.
content_html:
TL;DR: Hind limb dragging is a critical medical emergency often caused by spinal cord damage that requires immediate veterinary intervention to prevent permanent paralysis. Providing a video of your dog’s movement can help the medical team quickly assess the neurological severity and prepare for treatment.

What does it mean if my dog is dragging its back legs?

  • Hind limb dragging, also known as paresis or paralysis, typically indicates a significant disruption in communication between the brain and the lower body.
  • This condition is often caused by compression or damage to the spinal cord.
  • You may notice symptoms like 'knuckling' (walking on the tops of the paws), scuffing nails, or a total inability to stand on the rear limbs.

Is my dog dragging its hind limbs a veterinary emergency?

  • Yes, hind limb weakness or dragging is considered a high-priority medical emergency.
  • The condition is often extremely painful and can progress to permanent paralysis within hours if left untreated.
  • If your dog suddenly loses the use of their back legs, you should contact an emergency veterinarian immediately.
  • Prompt surgical or medical intervention is the best way to preserve your dog's quality of life and mobility.

How does providing a video or photo of my dog help the vet with triage?

  • A short video of your dog's movement (or lack thereof) provides the veterinarian with vital diagnostic information.
  • Adrenaline surges at the clinic can temporarily mask the severity of symptoms, making home videos essential for an accurate assessment.
  • A clear video helps the medical team determine the neurological grade of the issue before you arrive.
  • This allows the clinic to prepare necessary diagnostic tools, such as an MRI or CT scan, in advance.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

In dogs, dragging of the hind limbs can result from peripheral nerve injuries, commonly affecting the sciatic nerve or its branches due to pelvic fractures, intramedullary pin placement in the femur, or irritating injections near the nerve. Avulsion of caudal nerve roots can cause loss of sensation on the caudal surface of the limb and variable loss on the cranial surface, while cranial nerve root involvement spares sensation on the ventral paw. Sensory ganglioneuritis, seen in breeds like Siberian Huskies, can also cause ataxia in all limbs without paresis, decreased proprioception, weak reflexes, and reduced pain perception, and is diagnosed via electrodiagnostic testing and nerve biopsy. Amputation may be necessary due to dragging-related damage or self-mutilation, especially with complete nerve avulsion; recovery is possible in mild contusion cases.

Chapter: Neurology

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 1244)

faqs.question: What are common causes for a dog to drag its back legs?
faqs.answer: Common causes include Intervertebral Disc Disease (IVDD), Fibrocartilaginous Embolism (FCE), spinal trauma, or Degenerative Myelopathy.

faqs.question: How should I move my dog if they can't walk?
faqs.answer: Use a flat, stable surface like a piece of plywood or a rigid pet carrier to minimize spinal movement. Avoid lifting them under the belly, as this can worsen spinal injuries.

faqs.question: Can a dog recover from hind leg paralysis?
faqs.answer: Many dogs recover through emergency surgery or intensive medical management, but the prognosis depends heavily on how quickly they receive veterinary care and whether they still have deep pain perception.
